blocks.text: Help on built-in function print in module builtins: print(...) print(value,..., sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False) Prints the values to a stream, or to sys.stdout by default. Optional keyword arguments: file: a file-like object (stream); defaults to the current sys.stdout. sep: string inserted between values, default a space. end: string appended after the last value, default a newline. flush: whether to forcibly flush the stream. help(print) 1

Optional keyword arguments: file: a file-like object (stream); defaults to the current sys.stdout. sep: string inserted between values, default a space. end: string appended after the last value, default a newline. flush: whether to forcibly flush the stream. help(print) import pandas as pd pd.DataFrame()

blocks.text: # The pound sign marks the start of a comment. Python itself # ignores the comments, but they're helpful for anyone reading the code. for i in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]: print(i) # first line in "for i" block for j in [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]: print(j) # first line in "for j" block print(i + j) # last line in "for j" block print(i) # last line in "for i" block

Python Dynamic Typing
blocks.nearby_text_after: Python is both a strongly typed and a dynamically typed language. Strong typing means that variables do have a type and that the type matters when performing operations on a variable. Dynamic typing means that the type of the variable is determined only during runtime.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines the average value \(\bar{x}\) as the sum of the sample values \(x_i\) divided by the number of values \(n\). It is the standard mean used for central tendency.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
blocks.formula_explanation: This shows the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) equals one over \(n\) times the sum of the data values \(x_i\). It is the average of the observations.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
Numeric: Central Tendency with Mean
blocks.formula_explanation: This shows the sample mean \(\bar{x}\) as one over \(n\) times the sum of the values \(x_i\). It is the standard average of a set of observations.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance
Numeric: Spread with Variance Sample Variance
blocks.formula_explanation: This shows the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It is the standard unbiased variance formula for a sample.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance
Numeric: Spread with Variance Sample Variance
blocks.formula_explanation: This gives the sample variance \(s^2\) as the sum of squared deviations from the sample mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\). It measures spread in the data using the usual degrees-of-freedom correction.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance
blocks.nearby_text_after: but this is in ! dollars 2

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance Sample Variance
blocks.nearby_text_after: but this is in ! dollars 2
blocks.formula_explanation: This gives the sample variance \(s^2\). It is the sum of squared deviations from the sample mean \(\bar{x}\), divided by \(n-1\).

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Variance
Numeric: Spread with Standard Deviation
Numeric: Spread with Standard Deviation Sample Standard Deviation
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the sample standard deviation s. It is the square root of the average squared deviation from the sample mean, using n-1 in the denominator.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Numeric: Spread with Standard Deviation
Numeric: Spread with Standard Deviation Sample Standard Deviation
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula computes the sample standard deviation s. It takes the square root of the sum of squared deviations from the mean, divided by n-1.

blocks.formula_explanation: This formula gives the sample standard deviation, measuring spread around the sample mean \(\bar{x}\). It is the square root of the average squared deviation, using \(n-1\) in the denominator.

blocks.text: def find_dpi(w, h, d): """ https://medium.com/dunder-data/why-matplotlib-figure-inches-dont-match-your-screen-inches-and-how-to-fix-it-993fa0417dba w: width in pixels h: height in pixels d: diagonal in inches """ w_inches = (d ** 2 / (1 + h ** 2 / w ** 2)) ** 0.5 return round(w / w_inches) find_dpi(1920, 1080, 13.25) # approx what my native dpi is

blocks.text: # 5. repeat many times rand_mean_trip_diffs = [] iterations = 10_000 for i in tqdm(range(iterations)): permuted_trip_distances = trip_distances.sample(frac = 1,replace =False,random_state = i) rand_mean_weekend = permuted_trip_distances[:n_weekend].mean() rand_mean_weekday = permuted_trip_distances[n_weekend:].mean() rand_mean_trip_diffs.append(rand_mean_weekend -rand_mean_weekday) rand_mean_trip_diffs = np.array(rand_mean_trip_diffs) # convert list to numpy array rand_mean_trip_diffs[:5].round(2)

blocks.text: Wasserstein & Lazar, 09 Jun 2016] The ASA Statement on p-Values: Context, Process, and Purpose
blocks.nearby_text_before: Statistically Signicant?
blocks.nearby_text_after: Don't base your conclusions solely on whether an association or effect was found to be 'statistically signicant' (i.e., the p-value passed some arbitrary threshold such as p < 0.05). Don't believe an association/effect exists just because it was statistically signicant . Don't believe an association/effect is absent just because it was not stat. signicant .

blocks.text: def mab(arms = [],rewards = [],arm_names = [],epsilon = 0.4): n_arms = len(arms) # if not rewards: # for i in range(n_arms): # pulls.append(list) be_greedy = np.random.rand() > epsilon if not be_greedy: # randomly choose arm_idx = np.random.randint(0,n_arms) rewards[arm_idx].append(arms[arm_idx].rvs()) else: # be greedy reward_means = np.array([sum(x) / len(x) for x in rewards]) best_arms = np.where(reward_means == np.amax(reward_means))[0] arm_idx = best_arms[np.random.randint(0,best_arms.shape[0])] rewards[arm_idx].append(arms[arm_idx].rvs()) return rewards, be_greedy, arm_names[arm_idx] def print_mab_results(be_greedy,choice,rewards): print(f'greedy:{str(be_greedy):5s} choice:{choice} => ' + f"[{':'.join([str(round(sum(x) / len(x),1)) for x in rewards])}] " + f"| {','.join([str(x).ljust(20,' ') for x in rewards])}")

code_segments.text: def mab(arms = [], rewards = [], arm_names = [], epsilon = 0.4): n_arms = len(arms)
be_greedy = np.random.rand() > epsilon if not be_greedy:

reward_means = np.array([sum(x) / len(x) for x in rewards]) best_arms = np.where(reward_means == np.amax(reward_means))[0] arm_idx = best_arms[np.random.randint(0,best_arms.shape[0])] rewards[arm_idx].append(arms[arm_idx].rvs()) return rewards, be_greedy, arm_names[arm_idx] def print_mab_results(be_greedy,choice,rewards): print(f'greedy:{str(be_greedy):5s} choice:{choice} => ' + f"[{':'.join([str(round(sum(x) / len(x),1)) for x in rewards])}] " + f"| {','.join([str(x).ljust(20,' ') for x in rewards])}")

blocks.formula_explanation: This equation expresses the output $y_i$ as a weighted sum of multiple input variables for the $i$th example, plus an error term $\varepsilon_i$. The weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_m$ define the fitted plane or linear predictor.

Standardizing/Normalizing Features for Gradient Descent
blocks.formula_explanation: The visible formula defines z as the value x centered by subtracting the mean \bar{x}, then divided by the standard deviation s. It converts a feature into a standardized scale.

blocks.nearby_text_after: Zero mean and unit variance Standardizing/Normalizing Features for Gradient Descent

blocks.nearby_text_before: Global cost minimum
blocks.nearby_text_after: Zero mean and unit variance Standardizing/Normalizing Features for Gradient Descent

blocks.nearby_text_before: Global cost minimum
blocks.nearby_text_after: Standardizing/Normalizing Features for Gradient Descent

blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines a standardized variable z by subtracting the mean \(\bar{x}\) from \(x\) and dividing by the standard deviation \(s\). It rescales the feature to have zero mean and unit variance.

blocks.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression
blocks.formula_explanation: This defines the logistic function of x. It maps x to the sigmoid value 1/(1+e^{-x}).

blocks.nearby_text_after: Logistic Regression

blocks.nearby_text_before: Logistic Regression
blocks.formula_explanation: This defines the logistic function as a fraction with numerator 1 and denominator \(1+e^{-x}\). It maps a real input \(x\) to a value between 0 and 1.

blocks.text: def logistic(x,w1 = 1,w0 = 0): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(w0 + w1 * x))) x = np.linspace(-10,10,1000) # generate 1000 numbers evenly spaced between -10 and 10 fig,ax = plt.subplots(1,1,figsize = (10,5)) ax.plot(1 -x,logistic(x)); ax.set_xlabel('x');ax.set_ylabel('logistic(x)'); 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 -7.5 -5.0

code_segments.text: def logistic(x, w1 = 1, w0 = 0): return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-(w0 + w1 * x))) x = np.linspace(-10,10,1000)
# generate 1000 numbers evenly spaced between -10 and 10
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,1, figsize = (10,5)) ax.plot(1 -x,logistic(x)); ax.set_xlabel('x'); ax.set_ylabel('logistic(x)'); 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 -7.5 -5.0

blocks.text: proline: -3.464 hue: 0.488 X_cl = df_wine_2class[['proline','hue']] X_cl_zscore = X_cl.apply(lambda x: (x -x.mean()) / x.std()) logrm = LogisticRegression().fit(X_cl_zscore,y_cl) for (name,coef) in zip(X_cl.columns,logrm.coef_[0]): print(f'{name:10s}: {coef: 0.3f}')

code_segments.text: X_cl = df_wine_2class[['proline','hue']] X_cl_zscore = X_cl.apply(lambda x: (x -x.mean()) / x.std()) logrm = LogisticRegression().fit(X_cl_zscore,y_cl) for (name,coef) in zip(X_cl.columns,logrm.coef_[0]): print(f'{name:10s}: {coef: 0.3f}')

code_segments.text: with numpy arrays logrm = LogisticRegression().fit(X_cl_zscore.values,y_cl.values) fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,1, figsize = (6,6)) plot_decision_regions(X_cl_zscore.values, y_cl.values, clf = logrm, ax = ax); ax.set_xlabel(X_cl.columns[0]); ax.set_ylabel(X_cl.columns[1]); 1 2 3 4 5 6 Д A 4 ДД

blocks.text: def my_plot_decision_regions(X,y,model,figsize = (5,5),ax =None): '''Plot classifier decision regions, classification predictions and training data''' if not ax: fig,ax = plt.subplots(1,1,figsize = figsize) # use mlxtend plot_decision_regions model = model.fit(X.values,y.values) plot_decision_regions(X.values,y.values,model,ax = ax) ax.set_xlabel(X.columns[0]); ax.set_ylabel(X.columns[1]); def my_plot_regression(X,y,model,label = 'yhat',figsize = (5,5),ax =None): '''Plot regression predictions and training data''' # generate test data and make predictions X_test = np.linspace(X.iloc[:,0].min(),X.iloc[:,0].max(),1000).reshape(-1,1) model = model.fit(X.values,y.values) y_hat = model.predict(X_test) fig,ax = plt.subplots(1,1,figsize = figsize) ax.scatter(X, y, s = 20, edgecolor = "black", c = "darkorange", label = "data") ax.plot(X_test, y_hat, color = "cornflowerblue", label = label, linewidth = 2) ax.set_xlabel(X.columns[0]); ax.set_ylabel(y.name); ax.legend();

code_segments.text: def my_plot_decision_regions(X,y,model, figsize = (5,5), ax =None): '''Plot classifier decision regions, classification predictions and training data'''if not ax: fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,1, figsize = figsize)

model = model.fit(X.values,y.values) plot_decision_regions(X.values,y.values,model, ax = ax) ax.set_xlabel(X.columns[0]); ax.set_ylabel(X.columns[1]); def my_plot_regression(X,y,model, label = 'yhat', figsize = (5,5), ax =None): '''Plot regression predictions and training data'''
# generate test data and make predictions

X_test = np.linspace(X.iloc[:,0].min(),X.iloc[:,0].max(),1000).reshape(-1,1) model = model.fit(X.values,y.values) y_hat = model.predict(X_test) fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,1, figsize = figsize) ax.scatter(X, y, s = 20, edgecolor = "black", c = "darkorange", label = "data") ax.plot(X_test, y_hat, color = "cornflowerblue", label = label, linewidth = 2) ax.set_xlabel(X.columns[0]); ax.set_ylabel(y.name); ax.legend();

blocks.text: 0 59 1 71 Name: target, dtype: int64 df_wine = pd.read_csv('../data/wine_dataset.csv',usecols = ['alcohol','ash','proline','hue','class']) # rename 'class' as 'target', since class is a reserved python word df_wine = df_wine.rename({'class':'target'},axis = 1) df_wine_2c = df_wine[df_wine.target < 2] # only keep classes 0 and 1 X_2c = df_wine_2c[['proline','hue']] y_2c = df_wine_2c['target'] zscore = lambda x: (x -x.mean()) / x.std() X_2c_zscore = X_2c.apply(zscore,axis = 0) alcohol_2c_zscore = zscore(df_wine_2c.alcohol) y_2c.value_counts().sort_index()

code_segments.text: # rename 'class' as 'target', since class is a reserved python word

blocks.formula_explanation: This defines $R^2$ as one minus the ratio of squared prediction errors to the total squared variation around the mean. It measures how much variance the model explains compared with predicting the mean.

blocks.text: from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor dtr = DecisionTreeRegressor(max_depth = 10).fit(X[['proline']],y_r) r2_dtr = dtr.score(X[['proline']],y_r) print(f'{r2_lr =:0.2f}\n{r2_dtr =:0.2f}') X_query = pd.DataFrame({'proline':np.linspace(X.proline.min(),X.proline.max(),1000)}) y_dtr_pred = dtr.predict(X_query) y_lr_pred = lr.predict(X_query) fig,ax = plt.subplots(1,1,figsize = (8,4)) sns.scatterplot(x = X.proline, y = y_r,color = 'gray') ax.plot(X_query,y_dtr_pred,color = 'b') ax.plot(X_query,y_lr_pred,color = 'r');

code_segments.text: from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeRegressor dtr = DecisionTreeRegressor(max_depth = 10).fit(X[['proline']],y_r) r2_dtr = dtr.score(X[['proline']],y_r) print(f'{r2_lr =:0.2f}\n{r2_dtr =:0.2f}') X_query = pd.DataFrame({'proline': np.linspace(X.proline.min(),X.proline.max(),1000)}) y_dtr_pred = dtr.predict(X_query) y_lr_pred = lr.predict(X_query) fig, ax = plt.subplots(1,1, figsize = (8,4)) sns.scatterplot(x = X.proline, y = y_r, color = 'gray') ax.plot(X_query,y_dtr_pred, color = 'b') ax.plot(X_query,y_lr_pred, color = 'r');

(2*5*3 + 1 = 31) print(gscv.best_params_)

blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines precision as the fraction of predicted positives that are truly positive. It is TP divided by TP+FP.

Weighing Errors: Precision vs. Recall Out of the observations I predicted positive (TP+FP), how many are truly positive (TP)? Out of the truly positive (TP+FN), how many obsersevations did I predict positive (TP)?
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines recall as true positives divided by all truly positive cases, including false negatives. It measures how many actual positives were correctly identified.

blocks.section_title: How do we decide if something is positive or negative?
blocks.formula_explanation: This piecewise rule sets the predicted label \(\hat{y}_i\) to 1 when the conditional probability \(P(y_i=1\mid x_i)\) exceeds a threshold, and 0 otherwise. It is the standard way to turn a probability score into a binary classification decision.

blocks.text: Usually, threshold =.5, but it doesn't have to be.

code_segments.text: threshold =.5, but it doesn't have to be.

blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines the F1 score as twice the product of precision and recall divided by their sum. It combines the two measures into a single number, emphasizing balance between them.

blocks.section_title: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC
blocks.formula_latex: \text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{\text{negatives we got wrong}}{\text{all negatives}}
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines the false positive rate as FP divided by FP+TN. It also expresses the same quantity as the fraction of all negatives that were incorrectly classified as positive.

blocks.text: True Positive Rate (TPR) = Recall = = TP TP+FN positives we got right all positives

blocks.section_title: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC
blocks.formula_latex: \mathrm{True\ Positive\ Rate\ (TPR)} = \mathrm{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{\text{positives we got right}}{\text{all positives}}
blocks.formula_explanation: The formula defines true positive rate (recall) as TP divided by TP+FN. It also states this as the fraction of all actual positives that were correctly identified.

blocks.formula_latex: \text{False Positive Rate (FPR)} = \frac{FP}{FP+TN} = \frac{\text{negatives we got wrong}}{\text{all negatives}}
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines the false positive rate as the fraction of negative cases that were incorrectly predicted as positive. It is written equivalently as FP divided by FP plus TN, or as the number of negatives we got wrong over all negatives.

blocks.section_title: Paying attention to True Negatives: ROC
blocks.formula_latex: \text{True Positive Rate (TPR)} = \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN} = \frac{\text{positives we got right}}{\text{all positives}}
blocks.formula_explanation: This formula defines true positive rate, also called recall, as the fraction of actual positives that are correctly identified. It is shown as TP divided by TP+FN, or equivalently “positives we got right” over “all positives.”

blocks.text: def plot_roc(curves): fig,ax = plt.subplots(1,1,figsize = (6,6)) lw = 2 for fpr,tpr,model_name in curves: l1, = ax.plot(fpr, tpr, lw = lw, label = model_name) ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color = 'k', lw = lw, linestyle = '--') ax.set_xlim([0.0, 1.0]) ax.set_ylim([0.0, 1.05]) ax.set_xlabel('False Positive Rate') ax.set_ylabel('True Positive Rate') ax.set_aspect('equal', 'box') ax.set_title('Receiver operating characteristic example') ax.legend()

blocks.text: found alpha: 1.0, found l1_ratio: 0.5 dummy_synth train: 0.00 lr_synth train: 1.00 en_synth train: 0.95 dummy_synth test: -0.00 lr_synth test: 0.13 en_synth test: 0.24 from sklearn.datasets import make_regression from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNetCV X_synth,y_synth = make_regression(n_samples = 100, n_features = 200, n_informative = 10, random_state = 123) X_synth_train,X_synth_test,y_synth_train,y_synth_test = train_test_split(X_synth, y_synth, random_state = 123) dummy_synth = DummyRegressor(strategy = 'mean').fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) lr_synth = LinearRegression().fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) en_synth = ElasticNetCV(alphas = [.01,.1,1,100]).fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) print(f'found alpha: {en_synth.alpha_}, found l1_ratio: {en_synth.l1_ratio_}\n') print(f'dummy_synth train: {dummy_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'lr_synth train: {lr_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'en_synth train: {en_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}\n') print(f'dummy_synth test: {dummy_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'lr_synth test: {lr_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'en_synth test: {en_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}')

code_segments.text: found alpha: 1.0, found l1_ratio: 0.5 dummy_synth train: 0.00 lr_synth train: 1.00 en_synth train: 0.95 dummy_synth test: -0.00 lr_synth test: 0.13 en_synth test: 0.24

from sklearn.datasets import make_regression from sklearn.linear_model import ElasticNetCV X_synth, y_synth = make_regression(n_samples = 100, n_features = 200, n_informative = 10, random_state = 123) X_synth_train,X_synth_test,y_synth_train, y_synth_test = train_test_split(X_synth, y_synth, random_state = 123) dummy_synth = DummyRegressor(strategy = 'mean').fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) lr_synth = LinearRegression().fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) en_synth = ElasticNetCV(alphas = [.01,.1,1,100]).fit(X_synth_train,y_synth_train) print(f'found alpha: {en_synth.alpha_}, found l1_ratio: {en_synth.l1_ratio_}\n') print(f'dummy_synth train: {dummy_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'lr_synth train: {lr_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'en_synth train: {en_synth.score(X_synth_train,y_synth_train).round(2): 0.2f}\n') print(f'dummy_synth test: {dummy_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'lr_synth test: {lr_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}') print(f'en_synth test: {en_synth.score(X_synth_test,y_synth_test).round(2): 0.2f}')

This design choice is based on the idea that if you have two unknown or invalid values, you cannot denitively say they are the same.

blocks.text: 23.4 23.4 print(df_shop.price.mean().round(2)) print(df_shop.price.fillna(df_shop.price.mean()).mean().round(2)) # make a copy to keep our original df df_shop_impute = df_shop.copy() # fill missing price with mean of price df_shop_impute['price'] = df_shop.price.fillna(df_shop.price.mean()) # check to make sure all nulls filled assert df_shop_impute.price.isna().sum() == 0 assert df_shop_impute.price.notna().all() # also, that our mean hasn't changed assert df_shop.price.mean() == df_shop_impute.price.mean() assert df_shop.price.std() > df_shop_impute.price.std()

code_segments.text: values = [23.4 3.6] array([[23.4, 3.], [10.53, 2.], [19.77, 1.]]) 0 iris 1 NaN 2 carnation Name: favorite_flower, dtype: object array([['iris'], ['lilac'], ['carnation']], dtype=object) df_shop[['price','stars']].loc[20:22] from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer imp = SimpleImputer(strategy = 'mean').fit(df_shop[['price','stars']]) print(f'fill values = {imp.statistics_.round(2)}') imp.transform(df_shop.loc[20:22,['price','stars']]).round(2) df_shop.favorite_flower[:3] imp = SimpleImputer(strategy = 'most_frequent').fit(df_shop[['favorite_flower']]) imp.transform(df_shop.loc[:2,['favorite_flower']])

blocks.text: from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression df_shop_infer = df_shop.copy() not_missing = df_shop_infer.price.notna() missing = df_shop_infer.price.isna() lr = LinearRegression().fit(df_shop_infer.loc[not_missing,['stars']], df_shop_infer.loc[not_missing,'price']) df_shop_infer.loc[missing,'price'] = lr.predict(df_shop_infer.loc[missing,['stars']])

df_shop1 = pd.read_csv('../data/flowershop_data_with_dups_week8.csv') df_shop1 = df_shop1.drop_duplicates() df_shop1['purchase_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_shop1.purchase_date) df_shop1['price_missing'] = df_shop1.price.isna().astype(int) df_shop1['price'] = df_shop1.price.fillna(df_shop1.price.mean()) df_shop1['price_scaled'] = StandardScaler().fit_transform(df_shop1[['price']]) df_shop1['favorite_flower_missing'] = df_shop1.favorite_flower.isna().astype(int) df_shop1['favorite_flower'] = SimpleImputer(strategy = 'most_frequent').fit(df_shop1[['favorite_flower']])

code_segments.text: def adj_r2(model,X,y): n, m = X.shape return 1 -(1 -model.score(X,y)) * (n -1) / (n -m -1) 1 2 3

blocks.text: def plot_gallery(images, titles, h, w, n_row = 3, n_col = 4): """Helper function to plot a gallery of portraits""" plt.figure(figsize = (1.8 * n_col, 2.4 * n_row)) plt.subplots_adjust(bottom = 0, left =.01, right =.99, top =.90, hspace =.35) for i in range(n_row * n_col): plt.subplot(n_row, n_col, i + 1) plt.imshow(images[i].reshape((h, w)), cmap = plt.cm.gray) plt.title(titles[i], size = 12) plt.xticks(()) plt.yticks(()) plt.tight_layout() # plot the result of the prediction on a portion of the test set def title(y_pred, y_test, target_names, i): pred_name = target_names[y_pred[i]].rsplit(' ', 1)[-1] true_name = target_names[y_test[i]].rsplit(' ', 1)[-1] return 'predicted: %s\ntrue: %s' % (pred_name, true_name)

code_segments.text: def plot_gallery(images, titles, h, w, n_row = 3, n_col = 4): """Helper function to plot a gallery of portraits"""plt.figure(figsize = (1.8 * n_col, 2.4 * n_row)) plt.subplots_adjust(bottom = 0, left =.01, right =.99, top =.90, hspace =.35) for i in range(n_row * n_col): plt.subplot(n_row, n_col, i + 1) plt.imshow(images[i].reshape((h, w)), cmap = plt.cm.gray) plt.title(titles[i], size = 12) plt.xticks(()) plt.yticks(()) plt.tight_layout()
# plot the result of the prediction on a portion of the test set

def title(y_pred, y_test, target_names, i): pred_name = target_names[y_pred[i]].rsplit(' ', 1)[-1] true_name = target_names[y_test[i]].rsplit(' ', 1)[-1] return 'predicted: %s\ntrue: %s' % (pred_name, true_name)
